title: Python for Beginners
text:
1 Welcome to Python for Beginners
Welcome! This course is designed for complete beginners who want to learn real Python by writing lots of code.
crumbs: 1  Welcome

title: Python for Beginners
text:
1.1 What you will learn
By the end of the first part of the course you will be able to:

write and run Python programs
use variables and arithmetic
ask the user for input
make decisions with if
repeat code with loops
work with lists
crumbs: 1  Welcome

title: Python for Beginners
text:
1.2 How to use this course

Work through the lessons in order.
Type every example into Positron yourself.
Complete the exercises before opening the hints.
Only look at the solutions if you are really stuck.
Try the challenge exercises—they are where the real learning happens.
crumbs: 1  Welcome

title: Python for Beginners
text:
1.3 Course outline

Getting Started with Positron
Lesson 1 – Your First Python Program
Lesson 2 – Making Python Interactive
Lesson 3 – Making Decisions with if
Lesson 4 – Repeating Code with Loops
Lesson 5 – Lists and for Loops

Good luck, and have fun!
crumbs: 1  Welcome

title: 2  Getting Started with Positron
text:
3 Welcome to Positron
Positron is a modern programming environment (called an IDE, or Integrated Development Environment). It provides everything you need to write, run and organise your Python programs.
Don’t worry about learning every feature immediately. In this course, you will mainly use five parts of the window.


4 1. Explorer (left-hand panel)
The Explorer shows the files and folders in your project.
For example:
Python Course
│
├── lesson1.py
├── lesson2.py
├── guessing_game.py
└── notes.txt
Use the Explorer to:

open an existing program
create a new file
organise your work into folders



5 2. Editor (centre)
The large area in the middle is the Editor.
This is where you write your Python code.
Example:
name = input("What is your name? ")

print("Hello,", name)
You can have several files open at once. Each file appears as a tab across the top of the Editor.


6 3. Terminal (bottom)
The Terminal lets you type commands directly.
For example:
python lesson1.py
runs your program.
You can also create folders, move files and install packages from the Terminal.


7 4. Console
If you are using Python, you can also open a Python Console.
Unlike the Editor, the Console lets you try small pieces of code immediately.
For example:
3 + 4
or
print("Hello")
This is useful for experimenting.


8 5. Problems and Output
If your program contains an error, Positron may show it in the Problems or Output panel.
Read the error message carefully. It usually tells you:

where the error happened
what Python did not understand

Even experienced programmers make mistakes. Learning to read error messages is an important programming skill.


9 Running your first program

Open the Explorer.
Create a new file called lesson1.py.
Type:

print("Hello, world!")

Save the file.
Click the Run button, or open the Terminal and type:

python lesson1.py
If everything works, you should see:
Hello, world!


10 Keyboard shortcuts



Shortcut
What it does




Ctrl+S
Save the current file


Ctrl+Z
Undo


Ctrl+Shift+P
Open the Command Palette


Ctrl+`
Open or close the Terminal


Ctrl+/
Comment or uncomment selected lines





11 Good habits

Save your work often.
Give your files meaningful names.
Read error messages carefully.
Change only one thing at a time when fixing a bug.
Experiment! The best way to learn programming is by trying ideas yourself.



12 Looking ahead
After you are comfortable moving around Positron, you’re ready to begin Lesson 1 and write your first Python program.
crumbs: 2  Getting Started with Positron

Produce something like:
SHOP RECEIPT

Milk        R24
Bread       R18
Eggs        R52

Total:      R94
Choose your own products and prices.

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
5 Learning goals
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Estimated time: 45–60 minutes
crumbs: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
11.1 Mistake 1: Forgetting quotation marks
Wrong:
name = input(What is your name?)
Correct:
name = input("What is your name? ")
crumbs: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
11.2 Mistake 2: Forgetting int()
Wrong:
age = input("How old are you? ")
print(age + 1)
Correct:
age = int(input("How old are you? "))
print(age + 1)
crumbs: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
section: 11.3 Mistake 3: Typing words when Python expects a number
text:
11.3 Mistake 3: Typing words when Python expects a number
This will crash if the user types "twelve":
age = int(input("How old are you? "))
For now, type numbers using digits, like:
12
15
42
crumbs: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
12.1 Exercise 1

Print:
Hello, ...

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:

Print a sentence using their answer.
Example:

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
12.7 Exercise 7
Ask the user for a temperature in Celsius.
Convert it to Fahrenheit using:
Fahrenheit = Celsius × 9 / 5 + 32

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
12.8 Exercise 8
Ask the user how much money they have.
Ask how much they spend.
Print how much money they have left.
crumbs: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
13.1 Challenge 1: Personal introduction generator
Ask the user for:

name
age
favourite hobby
favourite food

Then print a short introduction.
Example:
This is Alex.
Alex is 15 years old.
Alex likes mountain biking.
Alex's favourite food is burgers.
crumbs: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
13.2 Challenge 2: Mini shop calculator
Ask the user for:

the price of item 1
the quantity of item 1
the price of item 2
the quantity of item 2

Print a receipt showing the two totals and the final total.
Example:
Item 1 total: R60
Item 2 total: R45
Final total: R105

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
13.21 Challenge 1
name = input("What is your name? ")
age = int(input("How old are you? "))
hobby = input("What is your favourite hobby? ")
food = input("What is your favourite food? ")

print("This is", name)
print(name, "is", age, "years old.")
print(name, "likes", hobby)
print(name + "'s favourite food is", food)
crumbs: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive

title: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive
text:
13.22 Challenge 2
price1 = int(input("Price of item 1: "))
quantity1 = int(input("Quantity of item 1: "))

price2 = int(input("Price of item 2: "))
quantity2 = int(input("Quantity of item 2: "))

total1 = price1 * quantity1
total2 = price2 * quantity2
final_total = total1 + total2

print("Item 1 total: R", total1)
print("Item 2 total: R", total2)
print("Final total: R", final_total)
crumbs: 4  Lesson 2: Making Python Interactive

title: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if
text:
6 Learning goals
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Estimated time: 45–60 minutes
crumbs: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if

title: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if
text:
12.1 Mistake 1: Forgetting the colon
Wrong:
if age >= 13
    print("Old enough")
Correct:
if age >= 13:
    print("Old enough")
crumbs: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if

title: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if
text:
12.2 Mistake 2: Forgetting indentation
Wrong:
if age >= 13:
print("Old enough")
Correct:
if age >= 13:
    print("Old enough")
crumbs: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if

title: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if
text:
12.3 Mistake 3: Using = instead of ==
Wrong:
if answer = "Paris":
    print("Correct")
Correct:
if answer == "Paris":
    print("Correct")
crumbs: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if

Ask the user for the temperature.

text:
13.8 Exercise 8
Ask the user for their favourite colour.

Blue is a calm colour.
If they type red, print:
Red is a strong colour.
Otherwise, print:

crumbs: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if

title: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if
text:
14.1 Challenge 1: Mini quiz
Create a three-question quiz.
Ask three questions, one at a time.
Keep a score variable.
Add 1 to the score for each correct answer.
At the end, print the final score.
Example:
Your final score is 2 out of 3.
crumbs: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if

title: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if
text:
14.2 Challenge 2: Adventure choice game
Create a tiny adventure game.
The user starts at a fork in the road.
Ask whether they want to go left or right.
If they go left, something happens.
If they go right, something else happens.
Then ask one more question and give a final ending.
Example:
You are standing at a fork in the road.
Do you go left or right?

title: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if
text:
14.21 Challenge 1
score = 0

answer1 = input("What language are we learning? ")

if answer1 == "Python":
    score = score + 1

answer2 = int(input("What is 6 * 7? "))

if answer2 == 42:
    score = score + 1

answer3 = input("Which planet do we live on? ")

if answer3 == "Earth":
    score = score + 1

print("Your final score is", score, "out of 3.")
crumbs: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if

title: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if
crumbs: 5  Lesson 3: Making Decisions with if

title: 6  Lesson 4: Repeating Code with Loops
text:
7 Learning goals
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
Estimated time: 45–60 minutes
crumbs: 6  Lesson 4: Repeating Code with Loops

title: 6  Lesson 4: Repeating Code with Loops
section: 14.1 Mistake 1: Forgetting to update the variable
text:
14.1 Mistake 1: Forgetting to update the variable
Wrong:
count = 1

while count <= 5:
    print(count)
This loop never stops, because count always stays 1.
Correct:
count = 1

while count <= 5:
    print(count)
    count = count + 1
crumbs: 6  Lesson 4: Repeating Code with Loops

title: 6  Lesson 4: Repeating Code with Loops
text:
14.2 Mistake 2: Indenting too much
Wrong:
count = 1

while count <= 5:
    print(count)
    count = count + 1
    print("Done")
This prints "Done" every time the loop runs.
Correct:
count = 1

while count <= 5:
    print(count)
    count = count + 1

print("Done")
This prints "Done" once, after the loop has finished.
crumbs: 6  Lesson 4: Repeating Code with Loops

title: 6  Lesson 4: Repeating Code with Loops
text:
14.3 Mistake 3: Using the wrong condition
This loop does not run at all:
count = 1

while count > 5:
    print(count)
    count = count + 1
Why? Because count > 5 is already false at the start.
crumbs: 6  Lesson 4: Repeating Code with Loops

section: 16.1 Challenge 1: Guess the number with hints
text:
16.1 Challenge 1: Guess the number with hints
Create a secret number.
Ask the user to guess it.
If their guess is too low, print:
Too low.
If their guess is too high, print:
Too high.
Keep going until they guess correctly.
Then print:
Correct!

title: 6  Lesson 4: Repeating Code with Loops
text:
16.2 Challenge 2: Multiplication practice
Ask the user:
What is 7 x 8?
Keep asking until they answer correctly.
Count how many tries they needed.
At the end, print something like:
Correct! You needed 3 tries.

title: 7  Lesson 5: Lists and For Loops
text:
16.1 Mistake 1: Forgetting the colon
Wrong:
for name in names
    print(name)
Correct:
for name in names:
    print(name)
crumbs: 7  Lesson 5: Lists and For Loops

title: 7  Lesson 5: Lists and For Loops
text:
16.2 Mistake 2: Forgetting indentation
Wrong:
for name in names:
print(name)
Correct:
for name in names:
    print(name)
crumbs: 7  Lesson 5: Lists and For Loops

title: 7  Lesson 5: Lists and For Loops
text:
16.3 Mistake 3: Expecting range(5) to include 5
for number in range(5):
    print(number)
This prints 0 to 4, not 1 to 5.
Use:
for number in range(1, 6):
    print(number)
if you want 1 to 5.
crumbs: 7  Lesson 5: Lists and For Loops

text:
18.2 Challenge 2: Compliment generator
Create a list of names.
For each name, print a different-looking compliment using the name.
Example:
Alex is becoming a strong Python programmer.
Ben writes careful code.
Chloe is great at debugging.
You can use the same sentence structure for each name if you want.
